Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered this semester. I'd try to carve out time around my work schedule and other responsibilities, but with limited resources and a lot going on, I couldn't always stick to a plan. I'd usually aim for late nights or early mornings when things were quieter. Sometimes I'd manage to get in a solid block of studying, other times I'd just squeeze in what I could between everything else. It wasn't ideal, but I made it work with what I had.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot depending on what was due and what else was happening in my life. When deadlines were approaching, I'd cram in more hours, but during quieter weeks I'd let things slide a bit. Being 26 and juggling multiple responsibilities meant I couldn't always prioritize coursework the way I wanted to. I'd say deadlines were really the main driver of when I studied intensively.
Q3: Usually what got me to log in was knowing an assignment was due soon or seeing a notification about something. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there was anything new posted that I might have missed. The gamified tasks actually helped motivate me to check in more often because there was something a bit more engaging about them. But honestly, if nothing urgent was happening, I might go days without logging in.
Q4: There were brief periods where logging in became more routine, especially when the gamified elements were active and I was trying to keep up with those. For a few weeks I'd check in almost daily just to see my progress on those tasks. But it never really stuck as a permanent habit because life would get in the way and the routine would break. The anxiety of falling behind sometimes pushed me to be more consistent, but that wasn't sustainable.
Q5: The reading materials and written content were most useful for me since I learn best through reading and writing. I appreciated being able to download PDFs and work through them at my own pace. The discussion posts were also helpful because seeing other students' perspectives sometimes clarified things I was confused about. The gamified tasks gave me small goals to work toward, which helped break things down when I was feeling overwhelmed.
Q6: I often skipped or minimized the video content, to be honest. Watching nearly three hours of video felt like a lot, and I found I could get the same information faster by reading. Some of the optional readings I'd ignore if I was pressed for time, figuring I'd come back to them later. I also didn't engage with every discussion thread because with my anxiety levels, putting my thoughts out there publicly felt stressful sometimes.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to assignments or exams. If it helped me understand something I'd be tested on, I was all in. The gamified tasks felt worthwhile because they gave me immediate feedback and a sense of accomplishment. Activities that felt vague or disconnected from my grades often got pushed to the bottom of my priority list because I just didn't have the bandwidth for extras.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt different because there was less pressure attached to them. Required ones came with that anxiety of consequences if I didn't complete them, so I'd force myself through even when I was exhausted. Optional stuff was easier to skip when I was overwhelmed, even if part of me knew it might help. Sometimes the optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford time-wise given everything else on my plate.
Q9: Absolutely, a lot of my studying happened offline. I'd download the readings and study from printed copies or saved files because I don't always have reliable internet access at home. I'd take handwritten notes and review those repeatedly, which is how I actually retain information best. I also used some free textbooks and resources I found online that weren't part of the official course materials.
Q10: There were many times I was deep into studying but the LMS wouldn't show it at all. I'd print out materials and study at the library or at home without logging in. Sometimes I'd work through problems on paper, reread my notes multiple times, or discuss concepts with a friend in person. All of that learning was real and meaningful, but it left no digital trace whatsoever.
Q11: Yes, this was actually my standard approach. I'd access a reading or resource once, download it immediately, and then never open it again in the LMS. I preferred having everything saved locally so I could study without needing internet. This meant my online activity looked minimal even when I was spending hours with those same materials offline. It was just more practical given my situation.
Q12: There were definitely rough patches, especially mid-semester when everything seemed to pile up at once. My anxiety would spike and I'd feel paralyzed, unable to start anything even though I knew I was falling behind. Work demands increased around that time too, and with limited financial resources, I couldn't cut back on work hours. Those weeks I felt like I was barely keeping my head above water.
Q13: All of those factors hit me at various points. Fatigue was constant because I was working and studying and not sleeping enough. Confusion about some course concepts made me avoid those topics rather than tackle them head-on. Competing responsibilities meant the LMS often got neglected in favor of more immediate concerns like work or family stuff. My engagement definitely suffered when life got complicated.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd open materials and just stare at them without anything sinking in. I'd be logged in, scrolling through content, but my mind was elsewhere worrying about bills or other stressors. Sometimes I'd watch a video and realize at the end I couldn't remember a single thing from it. Those sessions felt like wasted time even though they showed up as activity.
Q15: I postponed things more than I'd like to admit, especially the bigger assignments. The anxiety around them was so intense that avoiding them felt like relief, even though I knew it made things worse. I'd tell myself I'd start tomorrow when I was less tired or stressed, but tomorrow would come with its own problems. It was a cycle I struggled to break all semester.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire motivation system. I'd ramp up my LMS activity significantly in the days before something was due. Without a deadline looming, I'd often let things drift and focus on other life demands. It wasn't healthy, but that pressure was what got me to actually submit work. The gamified tasks with their mini-deadlines helped spread things out a bit more.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect on me. Positive feedback gave me a boost and made me want to engage more. Critical feedback sometimes triggered my anxiety and made me want to avoid that area of the course. I tried to use feedback constructively, but emotionally it was hard not to take it personally sometimes. When feedback was delayed, I'd already moved on mentally and it felt less useful.
Q18: The course structure was okay, but the flexibility was both a blessing and a curse for someone like me. Having seven online courses meant I had to self-manage a lot, and without strong external structure, I'd sometimes lose track of where I was. The gamified elements added some structure that helped. But overall, I needed more scaffolding than the course naturally provided.
Q19: The gamified tasks made engagement easier because they broke things into smaller, achievable pieces. Having clear deadlines helped too, even if I waited until the last minute. What made it harder was when materials weren't clearly organized or when expectations were vague. Also, heavy reliance on video content was tough for me since that's not my preferred learning style.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is anxious and checking things repeatedly without actually making progress. You might log in multiple times, click around, start things but not finish them because you're overwhelmed. It could also be someone who's confused and searching for help but not finding it. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity, especially when stress is involved.
Q21: That actually reflects my experience somewhat. A lot of my real learning happened offline with downloaded materials and handwritten notes. Someone looking at my LMS activity might think I wasn't engaged, but I was putting in the hours just not in ways the system could see. I think students who know how they learn best might not need to be constantly logged in to do well.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you're most active and what resources you click on. But they miss the quality of that engagement entirely. They can't tell if you actually understood something or if you were just clicking through while stressed out. They also miss all the offline work, the thinking, the discussions with peers that happen outside the platform.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks to check boxes. It's when I'm genuinely curious about a topic and want to dig deeper. For me, it also means feeling like I can manage the work without being crushed by anxiety. Real engagement is when learning feels meaningful rather than just stressful obligation.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I struggled with anxiety throughout the course, or how that affected my ability to engage even when I wanted to. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent with printed materials away from my computer. It also wouldn't show the mental effort it took just to keep going when resources were tight and life was demanding. My engagement was deeper than my login frequency suggests.
